Good morning, everyone. Let’s settle in and take a walk through the fundamentals of sound—how it’s generated, how it moves, and how we describe what we hear. I’m going to speak just as I would if we were sitting together in a lecture hall, so feel free to picture the diagrams in your mind as I talk.

---

### 1. How Sound Travels

We begin with the most basic idea: a sound source disturbs the particles of the medium around it—usually air, but it could be water, steel, or any other material that can carry pressure waves. Imagine a tiny piston moving back and forth; each forward stroke compresses the air in front of it, creating a region of higher pressure, while the backward stroke creates a rarefaction, a region of lower pressure. These compressions and rarefactions travel outward, much like a ripple spreads across a pond when you drop a stone.

As the wave propagates, the air molecules themselves don’t travel with the wave; they simply oscillate about their equilibrium positions, passing the pressure disturbance from one neighbor to the next. When this wave finally reaches a receptor—your ear drum or a microphone diaphragm—it pushes and pulls on that surface, causing it to move in sync with the pressure changes. That mechanical motion is the first step in the chain that leads to the perception of sound.

---

### 2. Frequency: The Rhythm of Vibration

Now let’s talk about frequency, the heartbeat of any sound. When a source vibrates periodically—think of a violin string—the motion repeats itself over and over. One complete cycle, from the start of a compression back to the same point in the next compression, is called a period, and the frequency is simply how many of those cycles occur each second. We measure it in Hertz (Hz), the reciprocal of the period.

Human hearing occupies roughly a twenty‑to‑twenty‑thousand Hertz band, which is an incredibly wide range. Some natural phenomena lie far outside this interval: barometric pressure changes occur at about ten‑to‑the‑minus‑5 Hz, while cosmic rays can reach frequencies on the order of 10²² Hz. Within our audible world, we have standardized reference pitches—most famously concert A at 440 Hz—which serve as anchors for tuning musical instruments.

Because pitch is perceived logarithmically, each octave represents a doubling of frequency. So A₄ is 440 Hz, A₅ is 880 Hz, and so on. This logarithmic relationship will be important later when we discuss how the ear interprets complex sounds.

---

### 3. Sound Properties: Wavelength, Diffraction, and Refraction

If frequency tells us how fast the wave cycles, wavelength tells us how far the wave travels during a single cycle. The two are linked by the speed of sound in the medium: wavelength equals speed divided by frequency. For example, a 20 kHz tone in air has a wavelength of roughly 1.8 cm, whereas a low‑frequency 20 Hz tone stretches out to about 1.7 meters.

Wavelength matters because it governs how sound interacts with the environment. Longer wavelengths diffract—bend—more readily around obstacles or through openings. That’s why you can still hear low bass notes from a distant speaker even if a wall blocks a direct line of sight, while the higher frequencies may be muffled. Refraction, on the other hand, occurs when the speed of sound changes across a boundary, causing the wavefront to bend. A classic example is the night‑time improvement in hearing outdoors: as the air cools, its density and temperature gradients cause sound to bend toward the ground, making distant noises more audible.

---

### 4. Factors That Influence Sound Propagation

Several physical properties of the medium influence how fast and how far sound travels. Temperature plays a big role; the speed of sound in air at 0 °C is about 1191 km/h, rising to roughly 1239 km/h at 20 °C. Musicians know this intimately—woodwind players warm their instruments because a warmer column of air lets the wave travel faster, subtly raising the pitch.

Elasticity and density also matter. Elastic media transmit pressure changes more efficiently, so sound moves through steel at about fourteen times the speed it does in air. Conversely, denser media tend to slow the wave down. This interplay helps explain why the same acoustic event can sound very different in a concert hall versus a steel chamber.

Attenuation, the gradual loss of energy as a wave travels, depends heavily on frequency. High‑frequency components are absorbed more quickly, which is why a distant thunderclap sounds low and rumbling; the higher frequencies are gone by the time the wave reaches us. Humidity adds another twist: moist air is less dense, which slightly raises the speed of sound, but it also increases attenuation because water vapor absorbs acoustic energy more readily.

---

### 5. Amplitude and the Decibel Scale

Amplitude is the measure of how much the air pressure is displaced from its normal state. Because the human ear can perceive sounds ranging from the faintest whisper to the roar of a jet engine—a dynamic range exceeding a million to one—we need a logarithmic way to express these differences. That’s where the decibel (dB) comes in.

When we talk about power quantities such as intensity, the formula is  

\[
\text{Level (dB)} = 10 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right).
\$

For amplitude‑related quantities like pressure, we use  

\[
\text{Level (dB)} = 20 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right).
\$

In both cases, the reference value \(P_2\ or \(A_2\ is the threshold of hearing. In terms of power, that threshold is about \(10^{-12}\ W m⁻²; in terms of pressure, it corresponds to roughly 20 µPa.

Let’s apply this to a practical example. A loud rock band might emit a sound power of \(10\ W m⁻². Plugging that into the intensity‑level formula, we get  

\[
10 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \times \log_{10}(10^{13}) = 10 \times 13 = 130\;\text{dB}.
\$

That’s a level you would certainly notice—and a good reminder of why hearing protection is essential at concerts.

Now, what happens when you have two identical sound sources, such as two motorcycle engines each producing an intensity level of 80 dB? First, we convert each level back to a power quantity. An 80 dB level corresponds to a power of  

\[
10^{\frac{80}{10}} \times 10^{-12} = 10^{-4}\;\text{W m}^{-2}.
\$

Two engines together give twice that power, \(2 \times 10^{-4}\ W m⁻². Converting back to decibels:

\[
10 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \times \log_{10}(2 \times 10^{8}) \approx 10 \times (8 + 0.301) \approx 83.0\;\text{dB}.
\$

Notice how the combined level is only about 3 dB higher—a useful rule of thumb is that adding another identical source raises the level by roughly 3 dB.

Because amplitude can be positive or negative, adding multiple sources directly in the amplitude domain can lead to cancellation. Power, being always positive, avoids that ambiguity, which is why we rely on power measurements when we sum sound sources.

---

### 6. Periodic Signals and Harmonics

All the sounds we love in music are, at their core, periodic waveforms. The simplest periodic waveform is a sine wave, which oscillates at a single fundamental frequency \(F_0\ Real instruments, however, produce more complex waveforms. These can be thought of as a series of sine waves at frequencies that are integer multiples of the fundamental—these are the harmonics.

If we take a note whose fundamental is 220 Hz, the second harmonic sits at 440 Hz, the third at 660 Hz, and so on. The particular mix and relative strength of these harmonics give each instrument its unique timbre—or "color." A clarinet and a violin playing the same pitch sound different because their harmonic spectra differ.

---

### 7. The Fourier Theorem

The relationship we just described is encapsulated by the Fourier theorem. In essence, any periodic waveform can be decomposed into a sum of sine waves—each with its own amplitude, frequency (a multiple of the fundamental), and phase. The Fourier transform is the mathematical tool that lets us move back and forth between a time‑domain representation (the waveform we see on an oscilloscope) and a frequency‑domain representation (the spectrum we see on an analyzer). This conversion is foundational for everything from digital synthesis to audio compression.

---

### 8. Phase: The Hidden Dimension

When we talk about a sine wave, we often write it as  

\[
y(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi),
\$

where \(\phi\ is the phase. Phase tells us where in its cycle the wave begins at time zero. To the human ear, a uniform shift in phase across the entire sound doesn’t change the perceived pitch or loudness—it’s essentially invisible. Yet phase becomes crucial when we combine multiple signals.

Consider two identical sine waves that are exactly out of phase by 180 degrees (or \(\pi radians). When they meet, the peaks of one line up with the troughs of the other, and they cancel each other out completely—a phenomenon called phase cancellation. Conversely, if the waves are in phase, their amplitudes add, giving us a louder sound. This principle underlies many engineering tricks, such as noise‑cancelling headphones, which inject an inverted version of ambient noise to reduce the perceived sound.

Phase also plays a role in creating standing waves. When a wave reflects off a surface and interferes with the incoming wave, certain points become nodes (no vibration) and others become antinodes (maximum vibration). Understanding where those points lie helps acousticians design concert halls, recording studios, and even speaker enclosures to avoid unwanted resonances.

---

### 9. Bringing It All Together

At this point, you’ve heard the story of sound from the moment a source disturbs the air, through the journey of pressure waves, to the way we quantify their frequency, wavelength, amplitude, and phase. You’ve seen how physical factors—temperature, elasticity, density, humidity—shape the speed and attenuation of those waves. You’ve also learned how the mathematics of Fourier lets us dissect any complex tone into its constituent sine waves, and why phase matters when we combine multiple sources.

All of these concepts interlock to form the toolbox we use when we design microphones, build concert venues, write digital audio effects, or simply tune a guitar. Whenever you hear a note, you can now imagine the invisible pressure ripple traveling through space, its frequency dictating pitch, its amplitude dictating loudness, and its harmonic content painting the unique timbral shade you recognize as a violin or a saxophone.

That concludes our overview of the fundamental concepts of sound. In the next session we’ll dive deeper into digital signal processing, where we’ll apply the Fourier transform in practice and explore how computers transform, synthesize, and manipulate these very waveforms we’ve just discussed.

Thank you for your attention—any questions before we wrap up?

### 1. How Sound Travels

We begin with the most basic idea: a sound source disturbs the particles of the medium around it—usually air, but it could be water, steel, or any other material that can carry pressure waves. Imagine a tiny piston moving back and forth; each forward stroke compresses the air in front of it, creating a region of higher pressure, while the backward stroke creates a rarefaction, a region of lower pressure. These compressions and rarefactions travel outward, much like a ripple spreads across a pond when you drop a stone.

As the wave propagates, the air molecules themselves don’t travel with the wave; they simply oscillate about their equilibrium positions, passing the pressure disturbance from one neighbor to the next. When this wave finally reaches a receptor—your ear drum or a microphone diaphragm—it pushes and pulls on that surface, causing it to move in sync with the pressure changes. That mechanical motion is the first step in the chain that leads to the perception of sound.

---

### 2. Frequency: The Rhythm of Vibration

Now let’s talk about frequency, the heartbeat of any sound. When a source vibrates periodically—think of a violin string—the motion repeats itself over and over. One complete cycle, from the start of a compression back to the same point in the next compression, is called a period, and the frequency is simply how many of those cycles occur each second. We measure it in Hertz (Hz), the reciprocal of the period.

Human hearing occupies roughly a twenty‑to‑twenty‑thousand Hertz band, which is an incredibly wide range. Some natural phenomena lie far outside this interval: barometric pressure changes occur at about ten‑to‑the‑minus‑5 Hz, while cosmic rays can reach frequencies on the order of 10²² Hz. Within our audible world, we have standardized reference pitches—most famously concert A at 440 Hz—which serve as anchors for tuning musical instruments.

Because pitch is perceived logarithmically, each octave represents a doubling of frequency. So A₄ is 440 Hz, A₅ is 880 Hz, and so on. This logarithmic relationship will be important later when we discuss how the ear interprets complex sounds.

---

### 3. Sound Properties: Wavelength, Diffraction, and Refraction

If frequency tells us how fast the wave cycles, wavelength tells us how far the wave travels during a single cycle. The two are linked by the speed of sound in the medium: wavelength equals speed divided by frequency. For example, a 20 kHz tone in air has a wavelength of roughly 1.8 cm, whereas a low‑frequency 20 Hz tone stretches out to about 1.7 meters.

Wavelength matters because it governs how sound interacts with the environment. Longer wavelengths diffract—bend—more readily around obstacles or through openings. That’s why you can still hear low bass notes from a distant speaker even if a wall blocks a direct line of sight, while the higher frequencies may be muffled. Refraction, on the other hand, occurs when the speed of sound changes across a boundary, causing the wavefront to bend. A classic example is the night‑time improvement in hearing outdoors: as the air cools, its density and temperature gradients cause sound to bend toward the ground, making distant noises more audible.

---

### 4. Factors That Influence Sound Propagation

Several physical properties of the medium influence how fast and how far sound travels. Temperature plays a big role; the speed of sound in air at 0 °C is about 1191 km/h, rising to roughly 1239 km/h at 20 °C. Musicians know this intimately—woodwind players warm their instruments because a warmer column of air lets the wave travel faster, subtly raising the pitch.

Elasticity and density also matter. Elastic media transmit pressure changes more efficiently, so sound moves through steel at about fourteen times the speed it does in air. Conversely, denser media tend to slow the wave down. This interplay helps explain why the same acoustic event can sound very different in a concert hall versus a steel chamber.

Attenuation, the gradual loss of energy as a wave travels, depends heavily on frequency. High‑frequency components are absorbed more quickly, which is why a distant thunderclap sounds low and rumbling; the higher frequencies are gone by the time the wave reaches us. Humidity adds another twist: moist air is less dense, which slightly raises the speed of sound, but it also increases attenuation because water vapor absorbs acoustic energy more readily.

---

### 5. Amplitude and the Decibel Scale

Amplitude is the measure of how much the air pressure is displaced from its normal state. Because the human ear can perceive sounds ranging from the faintest whisper to the roar of a jet engine—a dynamic range exceeding a million to one—we need a logarithmic way to express these differences. That’s where the decibel (dB) comes in.

When we talk about power quantities such as intensity, the formula is  

\[
\text{Level (dB)} = 10 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right).
\$

For amplitude‑related quantities like pressure, we use  

\[
\text{Level (dB)} = 20 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right).
\$

In both cases, the reference value \(P_2\ or \(A_2\ is the threshold of hearing. In terms of power, that threshold is about \(10^{-12}\ W m⁻²; in terms of pressure, it corresponds to roughly 20 µPa.

Let’s apply this to a practical example. A loud rock band might emit a sound power of \(10\ W m⁻². Plugging that into the intensity‑level formula, we get  

\[
10 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \times \log_{10}(10^{13}) = 10 \times 13 = 130\;\text{dB}.
\$

That’s a level you would certainly notice—and a good reminder of why hearing protection is essential at concerts.

Now, what happens when you have two identical sound sources, such as two motorcycle engines each producing an intensity level of 80 dB? First, we convert each level back to a power quantity. An 80 dB level corresponds to a power of  

\[
10^{\frac{80}{10}} \times 10^{-12} = 10^{-4}\;\text{W m}^{-2}.
\$

Two engines together give twice that power, \(2 \times 10^{-4}\ W m⁻². Converting back to decibels:

\[
10 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \times \log_{10}(2 \times 10^{8}) \approx 10 \times (8 + 0.301) \approx 83.0\;\text{dB}.
\$

Notice how the combined level is only about 3 dB higher—a useful rule of thumb is that adding another identical source raises the level by roughly 3 dB.

Because amplitude can be positive or negative, adding multiple sources directly in the amplitude domain can lead to cancellation. Power, being always positive, avoids that ambiguity, which is why we rely on power measurements when we sum sound sources.

---

### 6. Periodic Signals and Harmonics

All the sounds we love in music are, at their core, periodic waveforms. The simplest periodic waveform is a sine wave, which oscillates at a single fundamental frequency \(F_0\ Real instruments, however, produce more complex waveforms. These can be thought of as a series of sine waves at frequencies that are integer multiples of the fundamental—these are the harmonics.

If we take a note whose fundamental is 220 Hz, the second harmonic sits at 440 Hz, the third at 660 Hz, and so on. The particular mix and relative strength of these harmonics give each instrument its unique timbre—or "color." A clarinet and a violin playing the same pitch sound different because their harmonic spectra differ.

---

### 7. The Fourier Theorem

The relationship we just described is encapsulated by the Fourier theorem. In essence, any periodic waveform can be decomposed into a sum of sine waves—each with its own amplitude, frequency (a multiple of the fundamental), and phase. The Fourier transform is the mathematical tool that lets us move back and forth between a time‑domain representation (the waveform we see on an oscilloscope) and a frequency‑domain representation (the spectrum we see on an analyzer). This conversion is foundational for everything from digital synthesis to audio compression.

---

### 8. Phase: The Hidden Dimension

When we talk about a sine wave, we often write it as  

\[
y(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi),
\$

where \(\phi\ is the phase. Phase tells us where in its cycle the wave begins at time zero. To the human ear, a uniform shift in phase across the entire sound doesn’t change the perceived pitch or loudness—it’s essentially invisible. Yet phase becomes crucial when we combine multiple signals.

Consider two identical sine waves that are exactly out of phase by 180 degrees (or \(\pi radians). When they meet, the peaks of one line up with the troughs of the other, and they cancel each other out completely—a phenomenon called phase cancellation. Conversely, if the waves are in phase, their amplitudes add, giving us a louder sound. This principle underlies many engineering tricks, such as noise‑cancelling headphones, which inject an inverted version of ambient noise to reduce the perceived sound.

Phase also plays a role in creating standing waves. When a wave reflects off a surface and interferes with the incoming wave, certain points become nodes (no vibration) and others become antinodes (maximum vibration). Understanding where those points lie helps acousticians design concert halls, recording studios, and even speaker enclosures to avoid unwanted resonances.

---

### 9. Bringing It All Together

At this point, you’ve heard the story of sound from the moment a source disturbs the air, through the journey of pressure waves, to the way we quantify their frequency, wavelength, amplitude, and phase. You’ve seen how physical factors—temperature, elasticity, density, humidity—shape the speed and attenuation of those waves. You’ve also learned how the mathematics of Fourier lets us dissect any complex tone into its constituent sine waves, and why phase matters when we combine multiple sources.

All of these concepts interlock to form the toolbox we use when we design microphones, build concert venues, write digital audio effects, or simply tune a guitar. Whenever you hear a note, you can now imagine the invisible pressure ripple traveling through space, its frequency dictating pitch, its amplitude dictating loudness, and its harmonic content painting the unique timbral shade you recognize as a violin or a saxophone.

That concludes our overview of the fundamental concepts of sound. In the next session we’ll dive deeper into digital signal processing, where we’ll apply the Fourier transform in practice and explore how computers transform, synthesize, and manipulate these very waveforms we’ve just discussed.

### 1. How Sound Travels

We begin with the most basic idea: a sound source disturbs the particles of the medium around it—usually air, but it could be water, steel, or any other material that can carry pressure waves. Imagine a tiny piston moving back and forth; each forward stroke compresses the air in front of it, creating a region of higher pressure, while the backward stroke creates a rarefaction, a region of lower pressure. These compressions and rarefactions travel outward, much like a ripple spreads across a pond when you drop a stone.

As the wave propagates, the air molecules themselves don’t travel with the wave; they simply oscillate about their equilibrium positions, passing the pressure disturbance from one neighbor to the next. When this wave finally reaches a receptor—your ear drum or a microphone diaphragm—it pushes and pulls on that surface, causing it to move in sync with the pressure changes. That mechanical motion is the first step in the chain that leads to the perception of sound.

---

### 2. Frequency: The Rhythm of Vibration

Now let’s talk about frequency, the heartbeat of any sound. When a source vibrates periodically—think of a violin string—the motion repeats itself over and over. One complete cycle, from the start of a compression back to the same point in the next compression, is called a period, and the frequency is simply how many of those cycles occur each second. We measure it in Hertz (Hz), the reciprocal of the period.

Human hearing occupies roughly a twenty‑to‑twenty‑thousand Hertz band, which is an incredibly wide range. Some natural phenomena lie far outside this interval: barometric pressure changes occur at about ten‑to‑the‑minus‑5 Hz, while cosmic rays can reach frequencies on the order of 10²² Hz. Within our audible world, we have standardized reference pitches—most famously concert A at 440 Hz—which serve as anchors for tuning musical instruments.

Because pitch is perceived logarithmically, each octave represents a doubling of frequency. So A₄ is 440 Hz, A₅ is 880 Hz, and so on. This logarithmic relationship will be important later when we discuss how the ear interprets complex sounds.

---

### 3. Sound Properties: Wavelength, Diffraction, and Refraction

If frequency tells us how fast the wave cycles, wavelength tells us how far the wave travels during a single cycle. The two are linked by the speed of sound in the medium: wavelength equals speed divided by frequency. For example, a 20 kHz tone in air has a wavelength of roughly 1.8 cm, whereas a low‑frequency 20 Hz tone stretches out to about 1.7 meters.

Wavelength matters because it governs how sound interacts with the environment. Longer wavelengths diffract—bend—more readily around obstacles or through openings. That’s why you can still hear low bass notes from a distant speaker even if a wall blocks a direct line of sight, while the higher frequencies may be muffled. Refraction, on the other hand, occurs when the speed of sound changes across a boundary, causing the wavefront to bend. A classic example is the night‑time improvement in hearing outdoors: as the air cools, its density and temperature gradients cause sound to bend toward the ground, making distant noises more audible.

---

### 4. Factors That Influence Sound Propagation

Several physical properties of the medium influence how fast and how far sound travels. Temperature plays a big role; the speed of sound in air at 0 °C is about 1191 km/h, rising to roughly 1239 km/h at 20 °C. Musicians know this intimately—woodwind players warm their instruments because a warmer column of air lets the wave travel faster, subtly raising the pitch.

Elasticity and density also matter. Elastic media transmit pressure changes more efficiently, so sound moves through steel at about fourteen times the speed it does in air. Conversely, denser media tend to slow the wave down. This interplay helps explain why the same acoustic event can sound very different in a concert hall versus a steel chamber.

Attenuation, the gradual loss of energy as a wave travels, depends heavily on frequency. High‑frequency components are absorbed more quickly, which is why a distant thunderclap sounds low and rumbling; the higher frequencies are gone by the time the wave reaches us. Humidity adds another twist: moist air is less dense, which slightly raises the speed of sound, but it also increases attenuation because water vapor absorbs acoustic energy more readily.

---

### 5. Amplitude and the Decibel Scale

Amplitude is the measure of how much the air pressure is displaced from its normal state. Because the human ear can perceive sounds ranging from the faintest whisper to the roar of a jet engine—a dynamic range exceeding a million to one—we need a logarithmic way to express these differences. That’s where the decibel (dB) comes in.

When we talk about power quantities such as intensity, the formula is  

\[
\text{Level (dB)} = 10 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right).
\$

For amplitude‑related quantities like pressure, we use  

\[
\text{Level (dB)} = 20 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right).
\$

In both cases, the reference value \(P_2\ or \(A_2\ is the threshold of hearing. In terms of power, that threshold is about \(10^{-12}\ W m⁻²; in terms of pressure, it corresponds to roughly 20 µPa.

Let’s apply this to a practical example. A loud rock band might emit a sound power of \(10\ W m⁻². Plugging that into the intensity‑level formula, we get  

\[
10 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \times \log_{10}(10^{13}) = 10 \times 13 = 130\;\text{dB}.
\$

That’s a level you would certainly notice—and a good reminder of why hearing protection is essential at concerts.

Now, what happens when you have two identical sound sources, such as two motorcycle engines each producing an intensity level of 80 dB? First, we convert each level back to a power quantity. An 80 dB level corresponds to a power of  

\[
10^{\frac{80}{10}} \times 10^{-12} = 10^{-4}\;\text{W m}^{-2}.
\$

Two engines together give twice that power, \(2 \times 10^{-4}\ W m⁻². Converting back to decibels:

\[
10 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \times \log_{10}(2 \times 10^{8}) \approx 10 \times (8 + 0.301) \approx 83.0\;\text{dB}.
\$

Notice how the combined level is only about 3 dB higher—a useful rule of thumb is that adding another identical source raises the level by roughly 3 dB.

Because amplitude can be positive or negative, adding multiple sources directly in the amplitude domain can lead to cancellation. Power, being always positive, avoids that ambiguity, which is why we rely on power measurements when we sum sound sources.

---

### 6. Periodic Signals and Harmonics

All the sounds we love in music are, at their core, periodic waveforms. The simplest periodic waveform is a sine wave, which oscillates at a single fundamental frequency \(F_0\ Real instruments, however, produce more complex waveforms. These can be thought of as a series of sine waves at frequencies that are integer multiples of the fundamental—these are the harmonics.

If we take a note whose fundamental is 220 Hz, the second harmonic sits at 440 Hz, the third at 660 Hz, and so on. The particular mix and relative strength of these harmonics give each instrument its unique timbre—or "color." A clarinet and a violin playing the same pitch sound different because their harmonic spectra differ.

---

### 7. The Fourier Theorem

The relationship we just described is encapsulated by the Fourier theorem. In essence, any periodic waveform can be decomposed into a sum of sine waves—each with its own amplitude, frequency (a multiple of the fundamental), and phase. The Fourier transform is the mathematical tool that lets us move back and forth between a time‑domain representation (the waveform we see on an oscilloscope) and a frequency‑domain representation (the spectrum we see on an analyzer). This conversion is foundational for everything from digital synthesis to audio compression.

---

### 8. Phase: The Hidden Dimension

When we talk about a sine wave, we often write it as  

\[
y(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi),
\$

where \(\phi\ is the phase. Phase tells us where in its cycle the wave begins at time zero. To the human ear, a uniform shift in phase across the entire sound doesn’t change the perceived pitch or loudness—it’s essentially invisible. Yet phase becomes crucial when we combine multiple signals.

Consider two identical sine waves that are exactly out of phase by 180 degrees (or \(\pi radians). When they meet, the peaks of one line up with the troughs of the other, and they cancel each other out completely—a phenomenon called phase cancellation. Conversely, if the waves are in phase, their amplitudes add, giving us a louder sound. This principle underlies many engineering tricks, such as noise‑cancelling headphones, which inject an inverted version of ambient noise to reduce the perceived sound.

Phase also plays a role in creating standing waves. When a wave reflects off a surface and interferes with the incoming wave, certain points become nodes (no vibration) and others become antinodes (maximum vibration). Understanding where those points lie helps acousticians design concert halls, recording studios, and even speaker enclosures to avoid unwanted resonances.

At this point, you’ve heard the story of sound from the moment a source disturbs the air, through the journey of pressure waves, to the way we quantify their frequency, wavelength, amplitude, and phase. You’ve seen how physical factors—temperature, elasticity, density, humidity—shape the speed and attenuation of those waves. You’ve also learned how the mathematics of Fourier lets us dissect any complex tone into its constituent sine waves, and why phase matters when we combine multiple sources.

All of these concepts interlock to form the toolbox we use when we design microphones, build concert venues, write digital audio effects, or simply tune a guitar. ---

### 1. How Sound Travels

We begin with the most basic idea: a sound source disturbs the particles of the medium around it—usually air, but it could be water, steel, or any other material that can carry pressure waves. Imagine a tiny piston moving back and forth; each forward stroke compresses the air in front of it, creating a region of higher pressure, while the backward stroke creates a rarefaction, a region of lower pressure. These compressions and rarefactions travel outward, much like a ripple spreads across a pond when you drop a stone.

As the wave propagates, the air molecules themselves don’t travel with the wave; they simply oscillate about their equilibrium positions, passing the pressure disturbance from one neighbor to the next. When this wave finally reaches a receptor—your ear drum or a microphone diaphragm—it pushes and pulls on that surface, causing it to move in sync with the pressure changes. That mechanical motion is the first step in the chain that leads to the perception of sound.

---

### 2. Frequency: The Rhythm of Vibration

Now let’s talk about frequency, the heartbeat of any sound. When a source vibrates periodically—think of a violin string—the motion repeats itself over and over. One complete cycle, from the start of a compression back to the same point in the next compression, is called a period, and the frequency is simply how many of those cycles occur each second. We measure it in Hertz (Hz), the reciprocal of the period.

Human hearing occupies roughly a twenty‑to‑twenty‑thousand Hertz band, which is an incredibly wide range. Some natural phenomena lie far outside this interval: barometric pressure changes occur at about ten‑to‑the‑minus‑5 Hz, while cosmic rays can reach frequencies on the order of 10²² Hz. Within our audible world, we have standardized reference pitches—most famously concert A at 440 Hz—which serve as anchors for tuning musical instruments.

Because pitch is perceived logarithmically, each octave represents a doubling of frequency. So A₄ is 440 Hz, A₅ is 880 Hz, and so on. This logarithmic relationship will be important later when we discuss how the ear interprets complex sounds.

---

### 3. Sound Properties: Wavelength, Diffraction, and Refraction

If frequency tells us how fast the wave cycles, wavelength tells us how far the wave travels during a single cycle. The two are linked by the speed of sound in the medium: wavelength equals speed divided by frequency. For example, a 20 kHz tone in air has a wavelength of roughly 1.8 cm, whereas a low‑frequency 20 Hz tone stretches out to about 1.7 meters.

Wavelength matters because it governs how sound interacts with the environment. Longer wavelengths diffract—bend—more readily around obstacles or through openings. That’s why you can still hear low bass notes from a distant speaker even if a wall blocks a direct line of sight, while the higher frequencies may be muffled. Refraction, on the other hand, occurs when the speed of sound changes across a boundary, causing the wavefront to bend. A classic example is the night‑time improvement in hearing outdoors: as the air cools, its density and temperature gradients cause sound to bend toward the ground, making distant noises more audible.

---

### 4. Factors That Influence Sound Propagation

Several physical properties of the medium influence how fast and how far sound travels. Temperature plays a big role; the speed of sound in air at 0 °C is about 1191 km/h, rising to roughly 1239 km/h at 20 °C. Musicians know this intimately—woodwind players warm their instruments because a warmer column of air lets the wave travel faster, subtly raising the pitch.

Elasticity and density also matter. Elastic media transmit pressure changes more efficiently, so sound moves through steel at about fourteen times the speed it does in air. Conversely, denser media tend to slow the wave down. This interplay helps explain why the same acoustic event can sound very different in a concert hall versus a steel chamber.

Attenuation, the gradual loss of energy as a wave travels, depends heavily on frequency. High‑frequency components are absorbed more quickly, which is why a distant thunderclap sounds low and rumbling; the higher frequencies are gone by the time the wave reaches us. Humidity adds another twist: moist air is less dense, which slightly raises the speed of sound, but it also increases attenuation because water vapor absorbs acoustic energy more readily.

---

### 5. Amplitude and the Decibel Scale

Amplitude is the measure of how much the air pressure is displaced from its normal state. Because the human ear can perceive sounds ranging from the faintest whisper to the roar of a jet engine—a dynamic range exceeding a million to one—we need a logarithmic way to express these differences. That’s where the decibel (dB) comes in.

When we talk about power quantities such as intensity, the formula is  

\[
\text{Level (dB)} = 10 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right).
\$

For amplitude‑related quantities like pressure, we use  

\[
\text{Level (dB)} = 20 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right).
\$

In both cases, the reference value \(P_2\ or \(A_2\ is the threshold of hearing. In terms of power, that threshold is about \(10^{-12}\ W m⁻²; in terms of pressure, it corresponds to roughly 20 µPa.

Let’s apply this to a practical example. A loud rock band might emit a sound power of \(10\ W m⁻². Plugging that into the intensity‑level formula, we get  

\[
10 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \times \log_{10}(10^{13}) = 10 \times 13 = 130\;\text{dB}.
\$

That’s a level you would certainly notice—and a good reminder of why hearing protection is essential at concerts.

Now, what happens when you have two identical sound sources, such as two motorcycle engines each producing an intensity level of 80 dB? First, we convert each level back to a power quantity. An 80 dB level corresponds to a power of  

\[
10^{\frac{80}{10}} \times 10^{-12} = 10^{-4}\;\text{W m}^{-2}.
\$

Two engines together give twice that power, \(2 \times 10^{-4}\ W m⁻². Converting back to decibels:

\[
10 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \times \log_{10}(2 \times 10^{8}) \approx 10 \times (8 + 0.301) \approx 83.0\;\text{dB}.
\$

Notice how the combined level is only about 3 dB higher—a useful rule of thumb is that adding another identical source raises the level by roughly 3 dB.

Because amplitude can be positive or negative, adding multiple sources directly in the amplitude domain can lead to cancellation. Power, being always positive, avoids that ambiguity, which is why we rely on power measurements when we sum sound sources.

---

### 6. Periodic Signals and Harmonics

All the sounds we love in music are, at their core, periodic waveforms. The simplest periodic waveform is a sine wave, which oscillates at a single fundamental frequency \(F_0\ Real instruments, however, produce more complex waveforms. These can be thought of as a series of sine waves at frequencies that are integer multiples of the fundamental—these are the harmonics.

If we take a note whose fundamental is 220 Hz, the second harmonic sits at 440 Hz, the third at 660 Hz, and so on. The particular mix and relative strength of these harmonics give each instrument its unique timbre—or "color." A clarinet and a violin playing the same pitch sound different because their harmonic spectra differ.

---

### 7. The Fourier Theorem

The relationship we just described is encapsulated by the Fourier theorem. In essence, any periodic waveform can be decomposed into a sum of sine waves—each with its own amplitude, frequency (a multiple of the fundamental), and phase. The Fourier transform is the mathematical tool that lets us move back and forth between a time‑domain representation (the waveform we see on an oscilloscope) and a frequency‑domain representation (the spectrum we see on an analyzer). This conversion is foundational for everything from digital synthesis to audio compression.

---

### 8. Phase: The Hidden Dimension

When we talk about a sine wave, we often write it as  

\[
y(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi),
\$

where \(\phi\ is the phase. Phase tells us where in its cycle the wave begins at time zero. To the human ear, a uniform shift in phase across the entire sound doesn’t change the perceived pitch or loudness—it’s essentially invisible. Yet phase becomes crucial when we combine multiple signals.

Consider two identical sine waves that are exactly out of phase by 180 degrees (or \(\pi radians). When they meet, the peaks of one line up with the troughs of the other, and they cancel each other out completely—a phenomenon called phase cancellation. Conversely, if the waves are in phase, their amplitudes add, giving us a louder sound. This principle underlies many engineering tricks, such as noise‑cancelling headphones, which inject an inverted version of ambient noise to reduce the perceived sound.

Phase also plays a role in creating standing waves. When a wave reflects off a surface and interferes with the incoming wave, certain points become nodes (no vibration) and others become antinodes (maximum vibration). Understanding where those points lie helps acousticians design concert halls, recording studios, and even speaker enclosures to avoid unwanted resonances.

---

### 9. Bringing It All Together

At this point, you’ve heard the story of sound from the moment a source disturbs the air, through the journey of pressure waves, to the way we quantify their frequency, wavelength, amplitude, and phase. You’ve seen how physical factors—temperature, elasticity, density, humidity—shape the speed and attenuation of those waves. You’ve also learned how the mathematics of Fourier lets us dissect any complex tone into its constituent sine waves, and why phase matters when we combine multiple sources.

All of these concepts interlock to form the toolbox we use when we design microphones, build concert venues, write digital audio effects, or simply tune a guitar. Good morning, everyone. Let’s settle in and take a walk through the fundamentals of sound—how it’s generated, how it moves, and how we describe what we hear. I’m going to speak just as I would if we were sitting together in a lecture hall, so feel free to picture the diagrams in your mind as I talk.

### 1. How Sound Travels

We begin with the most basic idea: a sound source disturbs the particles of the medium around it—usually air, but it could be water, steel, or any other material that can carry pressure waves. Imagine a tiny piston moving back and forth; each forward stroke compresses the air in front of it, creating a region of higher pressure, while the backward stroke creates a rarefaction, a region of lower pressure. These compressions and rarefactions travel outward, much like a ripple spreads across a pond when you drop a stone.

As the wave propagates, the air molecules themselves don’t travel with the wave; they simply oscillate about their equilibrium positions, passing the pressure disturbance from one neighbor to the next. When this wave finally reaches a receptor—your ear drum or a microphone diaphragm—it pushes and pulls on that surface, causing it to move in sync with the pressure changes. That mechanical motion is the first step in the chain that leads to the perception of sound.

---

### 2. Frequency: The Rhythm of Vibration

Now let’s talk about frequency, the heartbeat of any sound. When a source vibrates periodically—think of a violin string—the motion repeats itself over and over. One complete cycle, from the start of a compression back to the same point in the next compression, is called a period, and the frequency is simply how many of those cycles occur each second. We measure it in Hertz (Hz), the reciprocal of the period.

Human hearing occupies roughly a twenty‑to‑twenty‑thousand Hertz band, which is an incredibly wide range. Some natural phenomena lie far outside this interval: barometric pressure changes occur at about ten‑to‑the‑minus‑5 Hz, while cosmic rays can reach frequencies on the order of 10²² Hz. Within our audible world, we have standardized reference pitches—most famously concert A at 440 Hz—which serve as anchors for tuning musical instruments.

Because pitch is perceived logarithmically, each octave represents a doubling of frequency. So A₄ is 440 Hz, A₅ is 880 Hz, and so on. This logarithmic relationship will be important later when we discuss how the ear interprets complex sounds.

---

### 3. Sound Properties: Wavelength, Diffraction, and Refraction

If frequency tells us how fast the wave cycles, wavelength tells us how far the wave travels during a single cycle. The two are linked by the speed of sound in the medium: wavelength equals speed divided by frequency. For example, a 20 kHz tone in air has a wavelength of roughly 1.8 cm, whereas a low‑frequency 20 Hz tone stretches out to about 1.7 meters.

Wavelength matters because it governs how sound interacts with the environment. Longer wavelengths diffract—bend—more readily around obstacles or through openings. That’s why you can still hear low bass notes from a distant speaker even if a wall blocks a direct line of sight, while the higher frequencies may be muffled. Refraction, on the other hand, occurs when the speed of sound changes across a boundary, causing the wavefront to bend. A classic example is the night‑time improvement in hearing outdoors: as the air cools, its density and temperature gradients cause sound to bend toward the ground, making distant noises more audible.

---

### 4. Factors That Influence Sound Propagation

Several physical properties of the medium influence how fast and how far sound travels. Temperature plays a big role; the speed of sound in air at 0 °C is about 1191 km/h, rising to roughly 1239 km/h at 20 °C. Musicians know this intimately—woodwind players warm their instruments because a warmer column of air lets the wave travel faster, subtly raising the pitch.

Elasticity and density also matter. Elastic media transmit pressure changes more efficiently, so sound moves through steel at about fourteen times the speed it does in air. Conversely, denser media tend to slow the wave down. This interplay helps explain why the same acoustic event can sound very different in a concert hall versus a steel chamber.

Attenuation, the gradual loss of energy as a wave travels, depends heavily on frequency. High‑frequency components are absorbed more quickly, which is why a distant thunderclap sounds low and rumbling; the higher frequencies are gone by the time the wave reaches us. Humidity adds another twist: moist air is less dense, which slightly raises the speed of sound, but it also increases attenuation because water vapor absorbs acoustic energy more readily.

---

### 5. Amplitude and the Decibel Scale

Amplitude is the measure of how much the air pressure is displaced from its normal state. Because the human ear can perceive sounds ranging from the faintest whisper to the roar of a jet engine—a dynamic range exceeding a million to one—we need a logarithmic way to express these differences. That’s where the decibel (dB) comes in.

When we talk about power quantities such as intensity, the formula is  

\[
\text{Level (dB)} = 10 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right).
\$

For amplitude‑related quantities like pressure, we use  

\[
\text{Level (dB)} = 20 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right).
\$

In both cases, the reference value \(P_2\ or \(A_2\ is the threshold of hearing. In terms of power, that threshold is about \(10^{-12}\ W m⁻²; in terms of pressure, it corresponds to roughly 20 µPa.

Let’s apply this to a practical example. A loud rock band might emit a sound power of \(10\ W m⁻². Plugging that into the intensity‑level formula, we get  

\[
10 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \times \log_{10}(10^{13}) = 10 \times 13 = 130\;\text{dB}.
\$

That’s a level you would certainly notice—and a good reminder of why hearing protection is essential at concerts.

Now, what happens when you have two identical sound sources, such as two motorcycle engines each producing an intensity level of 80 dB? First, we convert each level back to a power quantity. An 80 dB level corresponds to a power of  

\[
10^{\frac{80}{10}} \times 10^{-12} = 10^{-4}\;\text{W m}^{-2}.
\$

Two engines together give twice that power, \(2 \times 10^{-4}\ W m⁻². Converting back to decibels:

\[
10 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \times \log_{10}(2 \times 10^{8}) \approx 10 \times (8 + 0.301) \approx 83.0\;\text{dB}.
\$

Notice how the combined level is only about 3 dB higher—a useful rule of thumb is that adding another identical source raises the level by roughly 3 dB.

Because amplitude can be positive or negative, adding multiple sources directly in the amplitude domain can lead to cancellation. Power, being always positive, avoids that ambiguity, which is why we rely on power measurements when we sum sound sources.

---

### 6. Periodic Signals and Harmonics

All the sounds we love in music are, at their core, periodic waveforms. The simplest periodic waveform is a sine wave, which oscillates at a single fundamental frequency \(F_0\ Real instruments, however, produce more complex waveforms. These can be thought of as a series of sine waves at frequencies that are integer multiples of the fundamental—these are the harmonics.

If we take a note whose fundamental is 220 Hz, the second harmonic sits at 440 Hz, the third at 660 Hz, and so on. The particular mix and relative strength of these harmonics give each instrument its unique timbre—or "color." A clarinet and a violin playing the same pitch sound different because their harmonic spectra differ.

---

### 7. The Fourier Theorem

The relationship we just described is encapsulated by the Fourier theorem. In essence, any periodic waveform can be decomposed into a sum of sine waves—each with its own amplitude, frequency (a multiple of the fundamental), and phase. The Fourier transform is the mathematical tool that lets us move back and forth between a time‑domain representation (the waveform we see on an oscilloscope) and a frequency‑domain representation (the spectrum we see on an analyzer). This conversion is foundational for everything from digital synthesis to audio compression.

---

### 8. Phase: The Hidden Dimension

When we talk about a sine wave, we often write it as  

\[
y(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi),
\$

where \(\phi\ is the phase. Phase tells us where in its cycle the wave begins at time zero. To the human ear, a uniform shift in phase across the entire sound doesn’t change the perceived pitch or loudness—it’s essentially invisible. Yet phase becomes crucial when we combine multiple signals.

Consider two identical sine waves that are exactly out of phase by 180 degrees (or \(\pi radians). When they meet, the peaks of one line up with the troughs of the other, and they cancel each other out completely—a phenomenon called phase cancellation. Conversely, if the waves are in phase, their amplitudes add, giving us a louder sound. This principle underlies many engineering tricks, such as noise‑cancelling headphones, which inject an inverted version of ambient noise to reduce the perceived sound.

Phase also plays a role in creating standing waves. When a wave reflects off a surface and interferes with the incoming wave, certain points become nodes (no vibration) and others become antinodes (maximum vibration). Understanding where those points lie helps acousticians design concert halls, recording studios, and even speaker enclosures to avoid unwanted resonances.

### 9. Bringing It All Together

At this point, you’ve heard the story of sound from the moment a source disturbs the air, through the journey of pressure waves, to the way we quantify their frequency, wavelength, amplitude, and phase. You’ve seen how physical factors—temperature, elasticity, density, humidity—shape the speed and attenuation of those waves. You’ve also learned how the mathematics of Fourier lets us dissect any complex tone into its constituent sine waves, and why phase matters when we combine multiple sources.

All of these concepts interlock to form the toolbox we use when we design microphones, build concert venues, write digital audio effects, or simply tune a guitar. Whenever you hear a note, you can now imagine the invisible pressure ripple traveling through space, its frequency dictating pitch, its amplitude dictating loudness, and its harmonic content painting the unique timbral shade you recognize as a violin or a saxophone.
